questions.stem: A nurse is assessing a client who says: 'I've been thinking about ending my life. I have my grandfather's gun at home and I plan to use it this weekend when my family is away.' Which suicide risk category applies, and what is the immediate nursing priority?

options.text: Low risk; schedule outpatient follow-up within 2 weeks
Moderate risk; increase therapy frequency and provide a safety plan
High risk; initiate involuntary hospitalization and remove access to means immediately
Moderate risk; contact family members to monitor the patient at home

questions.rationale: This patient has a specific plan (gun), access to lethal means (gun at home), a timeframe (this weekend), and intent (plans to use it). This constellation — ideation + plan + means + timeframe — represents HIGH lethality risk. Immediate action: do not leave the patient alone, initiate emergency psychiatric evaluation, and facilitate involuntary hospitalization if the patient refuses voluntary admission. Means restriction (removing or securing the firearm) is critical. Outpatient follow-up (A) and home monitoring (D) are wholly inadequate for this risk level.

distractorRationales.a: The safer response addresses clinical safety risk; "Low risk; schedule outpatient follow-up within 2 weeks" moves care toward diagnostic reassurance before bedside safety action.
distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses clinical safety risk; "Moderate risk; increase therapy frequency and provide a safety plan" moves care toward routine care before bedside safety action.
distractorRationales.d: The cue "Means restriction (removing or securing the firearm) is critical" suggests clinical safety risk, so "Moderate risk; contact family members to monitor the patient at home" would not correct the active problem. Nursing safety depends on matching the response to the active clinical risk before routine care.

questions.takeaway: High suicide risk = specific plan + lethal means + timeframe + intent; immediate hospitalization and means restriction are required.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient with pneumonia who is receiving 4L/min via nasal cannula. The SpO2 is 91%. The nurse repositions the patient and administers the scheduled nebulizer treatment. Which finding would indicate the interventions were effective?

options.text: Respiratory rate decreases from 24 to 18 breaths/min and SpO2 increases to 95%
The patient reports less anxiety and requests to watch television
Adventitious breath sounds increase bilaterally after the nebulizer
The patient's skin color remains unchanged and RR remains at 24/min

questions.rationale: Effective respiratory interventions produce measurable objective improvements in gas exchange: SpO2 rising to ≥ 94-95% and respiratory rate normalizing toward < 20/min indicate improved ventilation and oxygenation. Decreased anxiety may reflect improved oxygenation but is subjective and non-specific. Increased breath sounds after a nebulizer can indicate mobilization of secretions (which is initially normal), not necessarily a therapeutic response. Unchanged SpO2 and RR indicate no meaningful improvement from the interventions.

distractorRationales.b: "The patient reports less anxiety and requests to watch television" treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. The cue "work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring)" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so airway and breathing intervention is needed before routine observation.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward delayed assessment instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "The SpO2 is 91%" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "The patient's skin color remains unchanged and RR remains at 24/min" moves care toward routine care before airway and breathing intervention.

questions.takeaway: Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization, and work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). Subjective comfort is supportive but secondary.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Oxygenation
visualRationale.caption: Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization, and work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). Subjective comfort is supportive but secondary.
visualRationale.conclusion: Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization, and work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). Subjective comfort is supportive but secondary.

questions.stem: A nurse is assessing vital signs in the pediatric unit. Which set of vital signs is within normal limits for a healthy 3-year-old toddler?
questions.rationale: Normal vital signs for a 3-year-old toddler: HR 80–130 bpm, RR 22–37 breaths/min, BP approximately 90–100/55–65 mmHg. HR 100, RR 26, BP 92/56 are all within expected ranges. Choice A has a bradycardic HR and adult-level BP. Choice C has tachycardia that could suggest distress, and RR 40 is elevated. Choice D has significant bradycardia. Key developmental milestone: vital signs normalize toward adult ranges with age — neonates have the highest HR and RR, lowest BP.

distractorRationales.a: "HR 68, RR 14, BP 120/80" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Pediatric VS by age: newborn HR 120–160, RR 30–60" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so airway and breathing intervention is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.c: "HR 145, RR 40, BP 80/48" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Pediatric VS by age: newborn HR 120–160, RR 30–60" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so airway and breathing intervention is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "toddler HR 80–130, RR 22–37" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Pediatric VS by age: newborn HR 120–160, RR 30–60; toddler HR 80–130, RR 22–37; school-age HR 70–110, RR 18–25; adolescent approaches adult norms.

visualRationale.title: Pediatric Nursing
visualRationale.caption: Pediatric VS by age: newborn HR 120–160, RR 30–60; toddler HR 80–130, RR 22–37; school-age HR 70–110, RR 18–25; adolescent approaches adult norms.
visualRationale.conclusion: Pediatric VS by age: newborn HR 120–160, RR 30–60; toddler HR 80–130, RR 22–37; school-age HR 70–110, RR 18–25; adolescent approaches adult norms.

questions.stem: A patient is scheduled for elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy at 8:00 AM. Per current ASA NPO guidelines, what are the correct restrictions for solid food and clear liquids?

options.text: Nothing by mouth after midnight — no exceptions for any type of food or liquid
Solid food stopped by midnight; clear liquids (including milk) allowed until 4 hours before surgery
Solid food (including non-clear liquids and milk) stopped at least 6-8 hours before; clear liquids (water, clear juice, black coffee) allowed until 2 hours before
No restrictions apply — laparoscopic procedures do not carry aspiration risk

questions.rationale: Current ASA NPO guidelines (2017, updated 2023): Solid foods, fried/fatty meals: NPO ≥8 hours. Light meals (toast, clear liquids): NPO ≥6 hours. Non-clear liquids including milk and formula: NPO ≥6 hours. Clear liquids (water, apple juice without pulp, clear carbonated beverages, black coffee or tea without cream, sports drinks): may be consumed up to 2 hours before elective procedures. Chewing gum and hard candy: treat as clear liquid — stop 2 hours before. These evidence-based guidelines replaced the outdated 'NPO after midnight' rule. The old rule increased patient discomfort, insulin resistance, and perioperative complications without reducing aspiration risk for clear fluids. High-risk patients (morbid obesity, GERD, pregnancy, gastroparesis, bowel obstruction) require individualized assessment and may need longer restriction.

distractorRationales.a: "Nothing by mouth after midnight — no exceptions for any type of food or liquid" misses the bedside risk. The cue "High-risk patients (morbid obesity, GERD, pregnancy, gastroparesis, bowel obstruction) require individualized assessment and may need longer restriction" points to unstable glucose physiology. Glucose instability can progress quickly, so medication timing and metabolic rescue come before routine follow-up.
distractorRationales.b: "Solid food stopped by midnight; clear liquids (including milk) allowed until 4 hours before surgery" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "High-risk patients (morbid obesity, GERD, pregnancy, gastroparesis, bowel obstruction) require individualized assessment and may need longer restriction" raises concern for unstable glucose physiology, so glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: The cue "High-risk patients (morbid obesity, GERD, pregnancy, gastroparesis, bowel obstruction) require individualized assessment and may need longer restriction" suggests unstable glucose physiology, so "No restrictions apply — laparoscopic procedures do not carry aspiration risk" would not correct the active problem. Glucose instability can progress quickly, so medication timing and metabolic rescue come before routine follow-up.

questions.takeaway: ASA NPO: solids ≥6-8 hrs before; clear liquids until 2 hrs before. 'NPO after midnight' is outdated and harmful. Clear liquids: water, clear juice (no pulp), black coffee/tea.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse is preparing to administer insulin glargine (Lantus) and insulin aspart (NovoLog) to a patient with type 1 diabetes before breakfast. Which action by the nurse requires IMMEDIATE correction?

options.text: Drawing up each insulin in a separate syringe
Mixing glargine and aspart in the same syringe before administration
Injecting the aspart subcutaneously 15 minutes before the meal
Rotating injection sites between the abdomen and thigh

questions.rationale: Insulin glargine (Lantus) is a long-acting basal insulin with a specific pH formulation. It should NEVER be mixed with any other insulin, including rapid-acting insulins. Mixing glargine with other insulins alters its pH, causing it to crystallize and unpredictably changes both insulins' absorption profiles. The result is an unreliable pharmacokinetic profile that can cause severe hypoglycemia or hyperglycemia. Each insulin must be drawn in a separate syringe and administered as separate injections. Drawing up separately is correct. Injecting aspart before meals (pre-prandial) is appropriate. Site rotation is correct practice.

distractorRationales.a: "Drawing up each insulin in a separate syringe" is accurate teaching, so it would not show the misunderstanding asked for in the stem. Drawing up each insulin in a separate syringe supports the teaching plan and medication safety.
distractorRationales.c: "Injecting the aspart subcutaneously 15 minutes before the meal" is accurate teaching, so it would not show the misunderstanding asked for in the stem. Injecting the aspart subcutaneously 15 minutes before the meal supports the teaching plan and medication safety.
distractorRationales.d: "Rotating injection sites between the abdomen and thigh" is accurate teaching, so it would not show the misunderstanding asked for in the stem. Rotating injection sites between the abdomen and thigh supports the teaching plan and medication safety.

questions.takeaway: Glargine (Lantus) = NEVER MIX with other insulins. It has its own pH and formulation. Mix-up can cause crystal formation and unpredictable glycemic effect. Use separate syringes, separate injections. Applies also to detemir (Levemir).
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for four patients on a medical-surgical unit. Which patient should the nurse assess first?

options.text: A 58-year-old with type 2 diabetes who reports feeling 'shaky' and has a blood glucose of 62 mg/dL
A 44-year-old post-appendectomy (day 2) requesting pain medication, pain rated 5/10
A 71-year-old with heart failure who gained 1 lb overnight and is on daily weights
A 33-year-old with a UTI who asks when the next dose of antibiotics is due

questions.rationale: The patient with a blood glucose of 62 mg/dL has symptomatic hypoglycemia ('shaky'), which is an acute, life-threatening condition requiring immediate intervention. Untreated hypoglycemia can rapidly progress to seizures, loss of consciousness, and brain injury. The other patients represent important but lower-acuity concerns: the post-op patient has manageable pain, the heart failure patient's weight gain needs assessment but is not immediately life-threatening, and the UTI patient's antibiotic timing is a scheduled task.

distractorRationales.b: The cue "Untreated hypoglycemia can rapidly progress to seizures, loss of consciousness" suggests unstable glucose physiology, so "A 44-year-old post-appendectomy (day 2) requesting pain medication, pain rated 5/10" would not correct the active problem. Glucose instability can progress quickly, so medication timing and metabolic rescue come before routine follow-up.
distractorRationales.c: The cue "Untreated hypoglycemia can rapidly progress to seizures, loss of consciousness" suggests unstable glucose physiology, so "A 71-year-old with heart failure who gained 1 lb overnight and is on daily weights" would not correct the active problem. Glucose instability can progress quickly, so medication timing and metabolic rescue come before routine follow-up.
distractorRationales.d: "A 33-year-old with a UTI who asks when the next dose of antibiotics is due" commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. The cue "The patient with a blood glucose of 62 mg/dL has symptomatic hypoglycemia ('shaky'), which is an acute, life-threatening" raises concern for unstable glucose physiology, so glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization is needed before diagnostic reassurance.

questions.takeaway: Apply the ABCs and Maslow's hierarchy: acute metabolic emergencies (hypoglycemia, airway compromise) always outrank scheduled pain requests, routine monitoring, and informational questions.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: The nurse assesses a patient following a traumatic brain injury. The patient opens eyes to voice, makes incomprehensible sounds, and withdraws from pain. What is this patient's Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) score?

options.text: GCS 7
GCS 8
GCS 9
GCS 10

questions.rationale: GCS components: Eye opening (E): spontaneous=4, to voice=3, to pain=2, none=1. Verbal (V): oriented=5, confused=4, inappropriate words=3, incomprehensible=2, none=1. Motor (M): obeys commands=6, localizes=5, withdraws=4, abnormal flexion=3, extension=2, none=1. This patient: Eyes to voice (3) + Incomprehensible sounds (2) + Withdraws (4) = GCS 7. GCS ≤8 = severe brain injury; generally indicates need for airway protection.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "Verbal (V): oriented=5, confused=4, inappropriate words=3, incomprehensible=2, none=1" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "GCS components: Eye opening (E): spontaneous=4, to voice=3, to pain=2, none=1" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: "GCS 10" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Score ≤8 = severe TBI, consider intubation for airway protection" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so airway and breathing intervention is needed before routine care.

questions.takeaway: GCS = E(1–4) + V(1–5) + M(1–6). Score ≤8 = severe TBI, consider intubation for airway protection.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A community health nurse is teaching a group of nursing students about levels of prevention. Which example correctly illustrates primary prevention?

options.text: Providing mammography screening to detect early breast cancer in women over 40
Administering the influenza vaccine to a healthy adult before flu season
Prescribing physical therapy for a patient recovering from a hip fracture
Conducting a colonoscopy to identify and remove pre-cancerous polyps

questions.rationale: Primary prevention: actions taken before disease occurs to prevent onset. Examples: immunization, health education, smoking cessation counseling, fluoridated water, condom use. Secondary prevention: early detection of existing disease (screening) — mammography, colonoscopy, blood pressure screening, Pap smear. Tertiary prevention: reducing disability and restoring function in patients with established disease — rehabilitation, cardiac rehab, chronic disease management. Mammography and colonoscopy are secondary prevention (detecting disease before symptoms). Physical therapy post-fracture is tertiary prevention.

distractorRationales.a: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of patient teaching and medication-safety reinforcement. The stem cue "A community health nurse is teaching a group of nursing students about levels of prevention" makes circulation or perfusion the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave circulation or perfusion untreated because it commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. "Administering the influenza vaccine to a healthy adult before flu season" fits the safer response to the cue "A community health nurse is teaching a group of nursing students about levels of prevention".
distractorRationales.d: "Conducting a colonoscopy to identify and remove pre-cancerous polyps" misses the bedside risk. The cue "Physical therapy post-fracture is tertiary prevention" points to circulation or perfusion. Teaching items should reinforce the specific medication, symptom, or safety rule rather than inventing a new emergency.

questions.takeaway: Primary prevention = before disease (vaccines, education); Secondary = early detection (screening); Tertiary = limit disability/restore function (rehab). Not to be confused with clinical levels of care.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse is completing an admission assessment on a 79-year-old patient transferred from a nursing facility. Which combination of factors identifies the HIGHEST fall risk?

options.text: Age >65 and female gender
History of fall in the past 6 months, taking 3 antihypertensive medications, and orthostatic hypotension (systolic drop >20 mmHg on standing)
Mild bilateral hearing loss and BMI of 29
Mild arthritis in both knees and occasional evening confusion

questions.rationale: The highest fall risk constellation combines: (1) Prior fall history — the single strongest predictor of future falls in the elderly; (2) Polypharmacy with antihypertensives — alpha-blockers, diuretics, and calcium channel blockers commonly cause orthostatic hypotension and syncope; (3) Documented orthostatic hypotension — directly causes dizziness and falls upon position change. Both the Morse Fall Scale and STRATIFY risk tool weight prior falls most heavily. Key interventions: bed in lowest position, non-skid footwear, call light within reach, scheduled toileting (most falls occur during nighttime toileting), orthostatic vital signs before ambulation, and urgent medication review. Age and gender alone are poor predictors without functional factors.

distractorRationales.a: The cue "The highest fall risk constellation combines: (1) Prior fall history — the single strongest predictor of future falls" suggests medication toxicity, so "Age >65 and female gender" would not correct the active problem. Medication safety depends on checking contraindications, labs, and adverse effects before giving or continuing the drug.
distractorRationales.c: "Mild bilateral hearing loss and BMI of 29" misses the bedside risk. The cue "The highest fall risk constellation combines: (1) Prior fall history — the single strongest predictor of future falls" points to medication toxicity. Medication safety depends on checking contraindications, labs, and adverse effects before giving or continuing the drug.
distractorRationales.d: "Mild arthritis in both knees and occasional evening confusion" misses the bedside risk. The cue "The highest fall risk constellation combines: (1) Prior fall history — the single strongest predictor of future falls" points to medication toxicity. Medication safety depends on checking contraindications, labs, and adverse effects before giving or continuing the drug.

questions.takeaway: Highest fall risk: prior fall + polypharmacy (antihypertensives) + orthostatic hypotension. Prior fall is the strongest single predictor. Target modifiable risk factors aggressively.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: An RN is caring for four patients and needs to delegate tasks. Which task is appropriate to assign to an unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP/CNA)?

options.text: Assess the breath sounds of a patient with new-onset dyspnea
Administer a tube feeding to a stable patient via an established G-tube
Ambulate a postoperative patient 24 hours after hip replacement surgery
Teach a diabetic patient how to perform foot care

questions.rationale: UAPs can perform tasks that are routine, repetitive, and do not require nursing judgment or assessment. Ambulating a stable postoperative patient 24 hours post-hip replacement is a safe, predictable task for UAP after the RN has assessed the patient's stability. Assessment of breath sounds (A) requires nursing judgment and cannot be delegated. Administering tube feedings (B) — in many states, enteral feeding via established G-tube can be delegated to trained UAPs, but the skill level required is greater than ambulation; this varies by state — ambulation is universally appropriate for CNA delegation. Patient education (D) is an RN responsibility requiring professional judgment.

distractorRationales.a: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "Assess the breath sounds of a patient with new-onset dyspnea" moves care toward ambulation, intake, or discharge teaching before airway and breathing intervention.
distractorRationales.b: "Administer a tube feeding to a stable patient via an established G-tube" moves to activity, intake, or discharge planning too early. The cue "Assessment of breath sounds (A) requires nursing judgment and cannot be delegated" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so airway and breathing intervention is needed before ambulation, intake, or discharge teaching.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward premature teaching instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "Patient education (D) is an RN responsibility requiring professional judgment" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: UAPs can perform routine, non-assessment tasks: ambulation, ADLs, vital signs on stable patients, I&O — assessment, teaching, and medication administration remain with the RN.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.
